event: Arab traders sailing the Indian Ocean become among the first to record knowledge of the islands later known as the Seychelles.
Portuguese explorer Vasco da Gama, on his second voyage to India, sights the Amirantes group, naming them in his own honor.
An English East India Company expedition aboard the Ascension makes the first recorded landing on the main Seychelles islands, finding them uninhabited.
French explorer Lazare Picault, sent by Mahé de La Bourdonnais from Mauritius, surveys the islands and names the main island after his patron.
France formally takes possession of the islands, naming them Séchelles after Jean Moreau de Séchelles, finance minister of Louis XV.
The first permanent settlers, a small group of French colonists and enslaved Africans, establish a colony on Sainte-Anne island.
Settlers begin cultivating spices and food crops on Mahé, founding the settlement that would become the capital.
The settlement of L'Établissement du Roi, later Victoria, is formally founded as the administrative center on Mahé.
British warships seize the islands during the French Revolutionary Wars, and administrator Jean-Baptiste Quéau de Quinssy negotiates repeated capitulations to preserve the colony.
Britain captures the Seychelles definitively as a dependency of Mauritius during the Napoleonic Wars.
The Treaty of Paris formally cedes the Seychelles to Britain, ending French colonial rule.
Slavery is abolished throughout the British Empire, ending legal slavery in the Seychelles and reshaping its plantation economy.
Africans freed from intercepted Arab slave ships begin to be landed and settled in the Seychelles, adding to the islands' population.
Ashanti king Prempeh and other exiled dignitaries are sent to the Seychelles, beginning the islands' long use as a British place of exile.
The Seychelles is separated from Mauritius and established as a distinct British Crown Colony with its own governor.
Seychellois recruits serving in the British Carrier Corps suffer heavy losses to disease during the East African campaign of World War I.
Limited elections introduce the first elected members to the colony's Legislative Council, dominated by the planter elite.
Britain rejects an appeal by the exiled Cypriot leader Archbishop Makarios III, who is detained in the Seychelles during the Cyprus emergency.
James Mancham founds the Seychelles Democratic Party and France-Albert René founds the Seychelles People's United Party, launching modern party politics.
Universal adult suffrage is introduced for the first time in elections to the Legislative Council.
Seychelles International Airport opens on Mahé, ending the islands' isolation and opening the era of mass tourism.
The Seychelles attains internal self-government with James Mancham as chief minister.
The Seychelles becomes an independent republic within the Commonwealth, with James Mancham as president and France-Albert René as prime minister.
The islands of Aldabra, Farquhar, and Desroches are returned to Seychelles from the British Indian Ocean Territory at independence.
France-Albert René seizes power in a coup d'état while Mancham is abroad, beginning a one-party socialist state.
A new constitution makes the Seychelles People's Progressive Front the sole legal party and confirms René's rule.
A mercenary force led by Mike Hoare, posing as tourists, attempts a coup but is repelled at the airport in a botched invasion.
An army mutiny is suppressed with the help of Tanzanian troops, consolidating President René's hold on power.
President René announces a return to multiparty democracy under domestic and international pressure.
A new multiparty constitution is adopted by referendum, and René wins the first competitive presidential election.
France-Albert René steps down after 27 years and is succeeded by Vice President James Michel.
The Indian Ocean tsunami strikes the Seychelles, causing deaths, flooding, and damage to coastal infrastructure.
A severe foreign-exchange and debt crisis forces the government to seek an IMF bailout and float the rupee.
Somali piracy in the western Indian Ocean prompts international naval patrols and anti-piracy operations based in the Seychelles.
President James Michel is re-elected as the country continues market-oriented economic reforms after the debt crisis.
Aldabra Atoll and Vallée de Mai remain showcase UNESCO World Heritage sites as Seychelles expands marine conservation efforts.
The opposition coalition Linyon Demokratik Seselwa wins control of the National Assembly for the first time in the country's history.
President James Michel resigns and hands power to Vice President Danny Faure.
Seychelles launches the world's first sovereign 'blue bond' to finance sustainable marine and fisheries projects.
Wavel Ramkalawan wins the presidency, marking the first transfer of power to the opposition since independence.
Seychelles becomes one of the world's most-vaccinated nations against COVID-19 while managing the pandemic's blow to its tourism economy.
